Lopez & Blanke provide the comprehensive anatomical and functional account of the thalamocortical vestibular system needed to ground ARC-017's VESTIBULAR stream tag. Key findings: (1) multiple thalamic nuclei relay vestibular signals (ventroposterior complex, ventroanterior-ventrolateral complex, intralaminar, posterior nuclear group) — these contain multisensory neurons integrating vestibular, proprioceptive, and visual signals; (2) the parieto-insular vestibular cortex (PIVC) is the core vestibular cortical region, with additional projections to somatosensory cortex (areas 2v, 3av), intraparietal sulcus, posterior parietal cortex (area 7), MST, frontal cortex, and hippocampus; (3) vestibular signals influence self-motion perception, spatial navigation, internal models of gravity, and bodily self-consciousness. This directly grounds the VESTIBULAR stream as distinct from both SELF_SENSORY/proprioception (joint/muscle state) and WORLD (exteroceptive content), with its own thalamo-cortical relay to parietal and MST areas that inform z_self's spatial frame.

Guirimand & Le Bars provide a comprehensive account of nociceptive anatomy that directly grounds ARC-027's claim about the HARM stream having its own dedicated sensory pathway. Key relevant points: (1) unmyelinated C fibres and fine myelinated Aδ fibres are the dedicated nociceptive afferents — not general sensory fibres; (2) ascending pathways run in the spinothalamic and spinoreticular tracts with relays at multiple supraspinal sites including parabrachial area, PAG, and critically the amygdala and hypothalamus as motivational/neuroendocrine targets; (3) the cingulate, insular and somatosensory cortices also receive nociceptive information — but via a secondary relay, not as the primary pathway. This maps directly to ARC-027: nociception has dedicated peripheral afferents, dedicated spinal pathways, and amygdala/hypothalamic projections that constitute the harm-detection substrate, with cortical processing as a secondary layer.

Li et al. demonstrate that the thalamic (low-road) and cortical (high-road) inputs to the lateral amygdala are not only parallel but mechanistically distinct: the thalamic pathway engages NMDA receptors in addition to AMPA, while the cortical pathway uses only AMPA. This means the two streams carry qualitatively different computational properties — the thalamic route can integrate signals over time via NMDA's voltage-dependent gating. For ARC-027, this deepens the case: the HARM stream (thalamic route) is not just anatomically parallel to E1 but uses a different synaptic mechanism, consistent with it being a distinct sensory channel with its own processing logic rather than a copy of the cortical signal.

Romanski & LeDoux provide the definitive experimental demonstration of the parallel low-road/high-road architecture. Selective lesions show that either the thalamo-amygdala pathway alone OR the thalamo-cortico-amygdala pathway alone is sufficient for fear conditioning; only destroying both disrupts conditioning. This directly grounds ARC-027's claim that the HARM stream operates via a parallel thalamic pathway that bypasses cortical (E1) processing: the sensory signal reaches the amygdala-equivalent independently of the cortical route. The experiment was conducted with auditory stimuli but the same circuit architecture applies to nociceptive/threat signals via the spinothalamic tract feeding posterior thalamic nuclei (VPM/Po) which project directly to the lateral amygdala.

Nath & Beauchamp demonstrate that STS functional connectivity with auditory cortex increases when auditory input is more reliable (less noisy), while connectivity with visual cortex increases when visual input is more reliable — and these changes occur even with rapidly fluctuating reliability. This is the neural correlate of precision-weighted multisensory integration. For MECH-103, this is the key piece: different exteroceptive modalities do not simply sum their contributions to E1's world latent — they contribute with weights proportional to their current reliability. This is exactly the precision-gating mechanism described in MECH-103. The dynamic nature (changes word-by-word) confirms this is an active, online weighting process rather than a fixed architecture.

Venezia et al. demonstrate a functional anterior-posterior gradient within STS: auditory speech produces largest BOLD in anterior STS, visual speech in posterior STS, and audiovisual (multisensory) stimuli in mid-STS. This spatial organisation shows that different exteroceptive modalities are processed via distinct streams that converge at a shared integration zone. The mid-STS preferentially responds to multisensory stimulation and speech over non-speech — consistent with MECH-103's claim that different modalities contribute differently to E1's world latent via their own encoder pathways. The gradient from modality-specific (posterior/anterior) to multimodal (mid) maps onto a multi-pathway encoder architecture converging on a shared latent.

Zhang et al. use direct intracranial sEEG recordings (42 patients, n=42 electrodes in STS/STG) to demonstrate that STS responses to audiovisual speech are 40% faster in latency and 18% larger in amplitude than auditory-only responses. Crucially, a subpopulation concentrated in mid-posterior STS responds to both auditory (71ms latency) and visual (109ms latency) speech — demonstrating that the STS integrates modality-specific inputs that arrive at different times. This directly grounds MECH-103: different exteroceptive modalities contribute to E1's world latent via convergent pathways with different temporal profiles and amplitudes. The precision-weighted fusion aspect (more reliable modality contributes more) is consistent with the STS acting as a reliability-sensitive integrator.

Canonical high-quality review in a premier journal. Directly documents hippocampal-cortical dialogue during NREM SWS as the mechanistic basis of memory consolidation; covers sharp-wave ripple replay, thalamo-cortical sleep spindles, and slow oscillation coordination. Maps cleanly onto MECH-030's core claim that offline consolidation uses hippocampal-cortical replay while suppressing active online processing. Confidence docked slightly because the REE sleep mode is explicitly V4 scope, adding transfer risk.

Seminal synaptic homeostasis hypothesis (SHY) paper; high source quality and wide replication. The core claim -- that waking net potentiates synapses and sleep globally downscales them to restore SNR -- maps directly onto MECH-030's precision recalibration and mode boundary recalibration functions. Confidence docked because SHY is a population-level homeostatic account, not a selectivity account, and REE needs selective residue recalibration (genuine harm preserved), which SHY does not explicitly address. V4 scope gap adds further transfer risk.

This is a theoretical integration paper (Hobson + Friston) casting REM sleep within active inference / predictive coding. Source quality is good -- both authors are prominent -- but the paper is more theoretical synthesis than primary data review, and the predictive coding interpretation of REM is not universally accepted. The key claim (REM as offline precision recalibration that updates the generative model's priors) maps onto MECH-030's mode boundary recalibration function. Confidence docked for theoretical nature of the source, contested status of the REM-predictive-coding account, and V4 scope gap.

Walker & Stickgold is a high-quality Annual Review paper covering sleep and memory consolidation broadly. The mixed direction reflects an important asymmetry: the paper supports the general consolidation function of sleep (alignment with MECH-030's core claim) but also documents failure modes -- maladaptive consolidation and intrusive replay -- that reveal the ARC-020 invariant ('genuine harm must not be erased') is not automatically satisfied by the biological sleep process. PTSD-related evidence shows that offline consolidation can amplify and distort, not just recalibrate, traumatic memory traces. This constitutes a genuine challenge to the REE assumption that sleep mode will reliably enforce the commit boundary and produce constructive recalibration.
failure_signatures: Offline consolidation can strengthen and distort traumatic memories rather than recalibrating them (PTSD model).
REM sleep intrusions during NREM can disrupt the orderly hippocampal-cortical transfer, impairing rather than supporting mode boundary recalibration.
Sleep-dependent consolidation of fear memories may lock in maladaptive action schemas, not just episodic content.
Fragmented sleep (REM disruption) impairs emotional memory processing, suggesting the offline phase is not robust to partial implementation.

entries.confidence_rationale: Canonical review articulating metastability as the core operating principle of large-scale brain dynamics: the brain transiently dwells in attractor-like states and transitions between them with characteristic dwell-time distributions. Directly supports MECH-047's claim of mode-commitment hysteresis. Mapping from whole-brain attractor dynamics to REE's three cognitive modes is structurally faithful. Main transfer risks are from network-level dynamics to the discrete mode-manager abstraction and from resting-state to task-engaged circuits.

Empirically rigorous task-fMRI paper showing that cognitive transitions are better described as continuous trajectories through a low-dimensional neural manifold than as discrete switches between labelled states. This directly challenges MECH-047's discrete mode-switching architecture and the sharp hysteresis gate. However, the paper also shows that neuromodulator activity (especially LC-NE proxy via pupillometry) predicts position along the manifold, which is partially consistent with MECH-047's arousal-gating component. Net: the continuous manifold finding weakens the discrete-mode assumption; the neuromodulator-position correlation provides partial support for the gating claim.
failure_signatures: cognitive_mode_transitions_are_continuous_not_discrete
no_sharp_switching_boundary_identified

entries.confidence_rationale: Empirical resting-state fMRI study using a hidden Markov model to identify transient brain states and their transition probabilities. The finding of hierarchical temporal organisation -- with fast micro-states nested inside slower macro-states -- directly supports MECH-047's claim that mode transitions are not instantaneous and carry switching costs. The hierarchical structure provides an empirical basis for distinguishing sub-mode fluctuations from genuine mode transitions. Main caveat: resting-state only; task-engaged dynamics and causal role of LC-NE are not directly addressed.

Canonical and highly cited review (>4000 citations) articulating LC-NE's role in adaptive gain modulation and the phasic/tonic LC firing distinction as a neural mechanism for switching between exploit and explore modes. Directly provides the neuromodulatory substrate for MECH-047's LC-NE arousal transition gate. Mapping to REE's mode-switching threshold is tight: phasic LC bursts = transition-enabling events; tonic LC baseline = background switching probability. Main caveat is that Aston-Jones & Cohen frame switching as exploit/explore rather than the three-mode REE taxonomy.

Major review directly addressing the functional role of theta-phase/gamma-amplitude coupling across human cortex, hippocampus, and prefrontal circuits. Provides both the mechanistic account (slow oscillation phase organises fast-oscillation bursts) and the cognitive function (enabling communication across spatial and temporal scales between hierarchically distinct systems). The packaging-for-higher-level-read-out mechanism is explicit and maps cleanly to MECH-089's ThetaBuffer. Slightly higher confidence than Lisman & Jensen (2013) for MECH-089 because this review covers the general cortical case, not just rodent hippocampal spatial coding, reducing species-transfer risk.

Influential review directly articulating the 'status quo' function of beta oscillations across motor and cognitive circuits — specifically distinguishing beta as a signal that maintains current state against change, not merely an idle rhythm. High source quality and conceptual precision; the framing directly maps to MECH-090's committed-sequence gating. Main transfer risk: the review argues beta reflects a top-down maintenance signal but does not directly distinguish internal-update blocking from output-propagation blocking, which is the REE-specific claim.

Foundational paper establishing the ERD/ERS framework and the 'idling rhythm' interpretation of beta: beta synchronizes when a cortical area is not engaged in active processing and desynchronizes during voluntary movement. This challenges MECH-090 in an important way -- if beta is primarily an idling/disengagement rhythm, then elevated beta during a committed sequence would indicate the motor cortex is *not* engaged, which contradicts the committed-maintenance gating account. Confidence 0.55 reflects genuine tension: the evidence against is real but the idling interpretation applies most clearly to sensorimotor beta, while MECH-090 targets prefrontal/BG beta during cognitive commitment -- a distinction subsequent work (Engel & Fries 2010; Helfrich & Knight 2019) explicitly makes.
failure_signatures: beta_as_idle_not_commitment
sensorimotor_beta_desynchronizes_during_action

entries.confidence_rationale: Keysers and Gazzola review strong evidence that the same cortical regions (premotor cortex, SII/parietal operculum, insula) are active both when an agent performs an action, emotion, or sensation AND when observing another perform the same -- i.e. self and other share neural representations. This directly challenges the clean z_self/z_world split: if the neural substrate for self-action and observed-action are overlapping rather than dissociated, strict computational separation of self and world streams is harder to maintain. Evidence is rated mixed rather than weakens because the overlap is partial and consistent with a competitive rather than fused representation, and because the mirror system primarily concerns action observation/understanding, not world-state modelling per se. The paper is high quality (>700 citations) and the phenomenon is robustly replicated.
failure_signatures: shared_self_world_representation
partial_overlap_in_z_self_z_world_substrates

entries.confidence_rationale: Wolpert and Kawato's MOSAIC model provides a well-established computational account of motor control via modular paired forward and inverse models. The forward model explicitly predicts the sensory consequences of self-generated actions without modelling world state. This is the canonical biological basis for a z_self stream (efference copy + forward prediction) that is computationally separable from a z_world stream. The paper is widely cited and the forward-model mechanism has substantial empirical support in cerebellar and parietal cortex. Confidence limited by the gap between motor control specifics and the broader latent-space architecture of SD-005.

Farrer and Frith directly dissociate the neural correlates of self-agency attribution from world-state/other-agency monitoring using fMRI. The anterior insula activates for self-caused actions; the right TPJ/inferior parietal cortex activates for other-caused (external) events. This double dissociation provides direct empirical support for separate neural substrates processing self-attribution (z_self domain) vs external-cause attribution (z_world domain). Well-cited study (>700 citations) in a high-quality empirical neuroscience venue. Confidence limited by the fact that the TPJ finding concerns other-person attribution specifically, not general world-state modelling, requiring an inferential step to z_world.

This is the canonical theoretical statement that sense of agency derives from a forward model comparator: the motor system issues an efference copy of the intended command, a forward model predicts the expected sensory consequence, and the comparator checks predicted against actual. Deviations are attributed to external causes; matches are attributed to self. This is exactly the architectural logic SD-003 formalises as causal_sig = E2(z_t, a_actual) - E2(z_t, a_cf). The clinical sections on delusions of control (schizophrenia passivity, utilisation behaviour) constitute natural experiments confirming that disrupting the forward model comparator produces misattribution of self-caused outcomes to external sources -- the precise failure mode SD-003 is designed to prevent. Confidence is moderated because the paper addresses the single-pass comparator (E2(a_actual) - observed), not the two-pass counterfactual difference SD-003 implements; the two-pass extension is inferential from this base.
failure_signatures: Patients with delusions of control report that their own actions feel alien or externally caused -- the forward model comparator is failing to match predicted to actual sensory consequence, so self-generated movements are misclassified as externally produced. This is the SD-003 failure mode: if E2 is miscalibrated, causal_sig will systematically misattribute the agent's own causal footprint.

Patients with anosognosia experience illusory awareness of movement despite sensory loss, driven by motor command streams alone -- suggesting the forward model can generate self-attribution independently of sensory feedback, which has implications for SD-003 in low-feedback environments.

The paper identifies parietal cortex as housing predicted and current state representations, and prefrontal/premotor as housing intended action representations -- disruption at either site produces distinct misattribution patterns, suggesting that the single forward model SD-003 implements may need to be decomposed in V4.

entries.confidence_rationale: This paper provides the formal theoretical grounding for why a learned forward model can in principle serve as a structural causal model (SCM) enabling interventional and counterfactual reasoning. SD-003 requires E2 to function as exactly this: a model that, given a state z_t and an action, faithfully represents P(z_harm_s_next | do(a)) and P(z_harm_s_next | do(a_cf)) -- interventional distributions that support the counterfactual difference causal_sig. Scholkopf et al. 2021 articulates why representation learning must target this causal structure (rather than merely correlational structure) to support transfer and generalisation -- directly relevant to SD-003's claim that E2 trained on harm-stream transitions can be used for counterfactual attribution rather than just one-step prediction. The paper identifies the core research gap: most causality work assumes causal variables are already known, whereas SD-003's E2_harm_s must learn them from harm-stream observations. Confidence is moderated because the paper addresses the static causal graph over observations rather than dynamic sequential causal attribution, and because the convergence conditions it identifies are for disentanglement tasks rather than sequential action-conditional forward models.
failure_signatures: Scholkopf et al. warn that most learned representations do not support interventional reasoning because they encode correlational structure rather than causal structure. If E2_harm_s is trained with a standard regression objective (minimising next-state prediction error), it may learn to encode correlational regularities between actions and harm outcomes rather than the underlying causal mechanism. In this case, E2(z_t, a_cf) would not correctly represent the interventional distribution P(z_harm_s | do(a_cf)), and causal_sig would be a biased estimate of the true causal contribution.

The paper argues that causal representation learning requires some form of interventional or multi-environment data to break the symmetry between observationally equivalent but causally distinct models. If SD-003's E2_harm_s is trained only on observational trajectories without explicit counterfactual perturbations, it may not learn a representation that supports the interventional computation causal_sig requires.

entries.confidence_rationale: COMA's counterfactual baseline is structurally the closest existing ML implementation to SD-003's causal_sig operation. COMA isolates an individual agent's causal contribution to a joint outcome by computing A(s, a) = Q(s, a) - sum_a' [pi(a'|s) * Q(s, a')], where the second term is the expected Q under the agent's marginal action distribution. This is a trained forward model (critic) evaluated twice -- once under the actual action and once under the counterfactual marginal -- with the difference extracted as the credit signal. The paper provides empirical convergence evidence in a cooperative multi-agent StarCraft benchmark, demonstrating that this counterfactual difference operation produces stable gradients and improves over non-counterfactual baselines. This is direct computational evidence that the forward model difference mechanism (SD-003's causal_sig) is a tractable, learnable, convergent credit assignment operation. Confidence is high for the computational claim; moderated by the multi-agent vs single-agent distinction.
failure_signatures: COMA's counterfactual baseline requires a centralised critic during training -- the Q-function must observe the full joint state-action space to correctly marginalise over other agents' actions. If the critic has limited information about the joint state, the counterfactual baseline is biased. In SD-003, the analogous risk is that E2 has limited observability of the state at the time of action, making causal_sig a biased estimate of the actual causal contribution.

The paper notes that COMA's counterfactual is over the marginal action distribution, not a specific counterfactual action. SD-003 uses a specific counterfactual action (a_cf), which is a different operation. The specific-counterfactual version requires a mechanism for selecting a_cf; COMA sidesteps this by marginalising. If counterfactual action selection in SD-003 is poorly specified, causal_sig will be sensitive to the choice of a_cf.

entries.confidence_rationale: This paper provides the clearest behavioral demonstration that the forward model output is actively subtracted from the perceived magnitude of self-caused stimuli before a causal judgment is made. Participants applying force to themselves systematically underestimate it relative to externally applied force of identical physical magnitude -- the forward model prediction is being compared against the sensory consequence and cancelling part of the perceived force. This is exactly the operationalisation of SD-003's causal_sig computation: the difference between what the agent's forward model predicted and what would have occurred without that action. The force escalation failure mode (each agent applies progressively more force in reciprocal conflict because they perceive their own force as smaller than their partner's) is a vivid empirical illustration of what happens when the forward model comparison is not completed by a counterfactual: the agent cannot distinguish 'I caused this' from 'this happened to me'. Confidence is moderated by the modality gap (tactile force vs. harm proximity in REE) and by the single-pass nature of the demonstrated mechanism.
failure_signatures: Force escalation cycles emerge when the forward model comparison is complete for self-produced stimuli (which are perceived as smaller) but no counterfactual is available for partner-produced stimuli -- the asymmetry in perceived magnitude drives runaway escalation. This maps onto an SD-003 failure mode: an agent without a calibrated counterfactual comparison would systematically underestimate its own harm contribution relative to external harm events, potentially missing its own causal footprint entirely.

The cancellation effect is specific to the predicted sensory consequence of the movement -- it does not generalise to stimuli outside the forward model's prediction window. This implies that E2 in SD-003 must be trained on the statistical relationship between specific actions and their harm-stream consequences; an undertrained E2 would fail to cancel its own outputs and would misattribute self-caused harm as external.

entries.confidence_rationale: High-impact Nature Neuroscience paper establishing that the cerebellum predicts the specific sensory consequences of movement and provides the cancellation signal — the forward model framework underlying SD-007's ReafferencePredictor. fMRI shows reduced somatosensory cortex activity and reduced cerebellar activity for self-produced vs. externally produced touch. The Wolpert group's forward model interpretation (cerebellum as internal model computing sensory prediction) is exactly the computational form implemented in SD-007. Confidence is moderated by modality transfer (tactile -> visual) and by the coarse spatial resolution of fMRI relative to single-unit findings.
failure_signatures: The cancellation is shown to be specific to self-produced stimuli with predictable sensory consequences — externally produced stimuli of identical physical parameters are perceived as significantly more intense. This implies the predictor must be trained on the specific statistical relationship between actions and sensory outcomes; an untrained or poorly calibrated predictor would fail to cancel.

The cerebellum shows less activation during the movement that generates the tactile stimulus than during a movement that does not — suggesting the cancellation signal is computed in relation to the predicted sensory consequence of the specific action, not movement in general.

entries.confidence_rationale: Authoritative review by the Cullen group synthesising circuit-level evidence for motor-signal-based reafference cancellation across somatosensory, vestibular, auditory, and visual systems. Directly relevant to SD-007: the paper establishes (a) the generality of the principle, (b) that cancellation occurs at the earliest stages of central processing, with cerebellum central, and (c) that visual processing implements cancellation at cortical rather than subcortical stages — matching SD-007's placement of the ReafferencePredictor at the latent encoding level rather than the raw sensory level. Low transfer risk because the review explicitly covers visual processing.
failure_signatures: The review notes important differences across sensory systems: visual reafference cancellation operates at higher cortical stages than vestibular/somatosensory suppression, implying that the REE architecture's single latent-level subtraction is an abstraction of a multi-stage biological process.

The review emphasises that the brain continuously calibrates the relationship between motor signals and sensory feedback — a static predictor without ongoing calibration would diverge, a failure mode not directly addressed in SD-007's current implementation.

entries.confidence_rationale: Behavioural study establishing that self-generated sensory attenuation degrades parametrically with temporal delay and spatial mismatch between motor action and sensory outcome — the attenuation is proportional to the error between the forward model prediction and actual sensory feedback. This provides direct support for SD-007's design choice that the predictor input must be the world state (z_world_raw_prev) rather than the body state (z_self_prev): the cancellation only works when there is spatio-temporal correspondence between the action and the specific sensory change it produces. Confidence is moderated by the tactile modality and the human psychophysics methodology.
failure_signatures: Attenuation is graded by temporal delay: increasing delay between action and sensory outcome reduces attenuation, suggesting temporal correspondence is a prerequisite. A ReafferencePredictor operating on a stale prior state (large effective delay) would lose its cancellation effectiveness.

Trajectory perturbation (spatial mismatch between intended and actual sensory change direction) also reduces attenuation, implying the predictor must encode the directionality of the expected sensory change — a purely scalar or action-type-only predictor would be insufficient.

entries.confidence_rationale: High source quality (J Neurosci, single-unit in macaque, widely cited). Strong mapping fidelity: the paper directly demonstrates that the motor command (efference copy of neck activation), not proprioceptive feedback, is the effective input for cancelling self-generated vestibular reafference — directly analogous to SD-007's design decision that z_world_raw_prev (not z_self_prev) must drive the ReafferencePredictor. Transfer risk is moderate: this is the vestibular system, while SD-007 targets visual/optic flow, but the reafference cancellation principle is shown to be general across sensory modalities.
failure_signatures: Cancellation is gated by neck motor command activation — if no neck muscle activation occurs (e.g., arm-driven whole-body rotation), neurons encode head motion normally, suggesting the efference copy mechanism is specific to the activated musculature, not a global self-motion suppression.
Proprioceptive input alone does not produce the cancellation effect — passive stretch of neck muscles during externally driven head motion does not gate vestibular responses.

entries.confidence_rationale: Strong mapping fidelity: predatory imminence continuum theory is the ethological formalisation of ARC-024's gradient structure claim, providing both the theoretical grounding and neural circuit detail. Pre-encounter, post-encounter, and circa-strike modes directly instantiate the gradient: behaviour form and vigour both change continuously as a function of perceived threat distance, before contact occurs. Source quality is solid (Fanselow is the originator of PIC theory, open-access PMC journal). Transfer risk is moderate (primarily animal literature; human data exists but is secondary).
failure_signatures: PIC theory describes three discrete modes rather than a fully continuous gradient; the transitions between modes are threshold-like, which could be interpreted as arguing for partial discreteness rather than pure continuous gradient structure.
Primarily rodent literature; human PIC research (including Mobbs 2007) is referenced but not the primary evidence base of this review.
The review's focus is on sustained threat and BST/hippocampal circuits rather than the gradient structure per se; the gradient argument must be inferred from the broader PIC framework.

entries.confidence_rationale: Excellent source quality (Nature, human fMRI, Dayan/Dolan/Friston collaboration). Mapping fidelity is moderate: the paper demonstrates a *temporal* gradient (harm prediction building up as a cue sequence unfolds before the painful event) which is the temporal analogue of ARC-024's spatial gradient. The brain tracks harm signal magnitude across the sequence of cues leading up to contact — supporting the proxy-gradient claim. Transfer risk is elevated because temporal prediction gradients and spatial proximity gradients are distinct mechanisms, even if both support the broader claim.
failure_signatures: Temporal prediction gradients (cue1 -> cue2 -> pain) are structurally analogous to but not identical with spatial proximity gradients (distance to hazard).
Ventral striatum/insula activity reflects reward-prediction-error signals, not the harm gradient field directly; the mapping to CausalGridWorldV2's hazard_field requires additional theoretical steps.
Study used electrical pain stimuli; generalisability to naturalistic harm sources is uncertain.

entries.confidence_rationale: High source quality (Science, human fMRI) and strong mapping fidelity: vmPFC-to-PAG shift is a direct neural instantiation of ARC-024's gradient structure. Harm signal magnitude increases continuously as threat proximity increases, before contact occurs. Modest transfer risk because the virtual-threat paradigm may not capture all naturalistic gradient properties.
failure_signatures: Small sample (n=14) limits generalisability of the vmPFC-PAG gradient finding.
Virtual predator paradigm with controlled shock delivery may produce cleaner gradients than real-world harm sources, potentially overestimating gradient smoothness.
Shock magnitude was known to participants; real-world harm is uncertain, which could alter the shape of the gradient.

entries.confidence_rationale: High mapping fidelity for the precision-weighting half of ARC-016's circuit: dopamine modulates prediction error precision (inverse variance of expectations), which in the REE mapping corresponds directly to E3-derived variance setting the weight of prediction errors that drive action selection. Source quality is strong (PLoS Comp Bio, Friston lab). Transfer risk is moderate because the paper models motor action affordances specifically, not a general E3→BetaGate→action_selection circuit.
failure_signatures: The model addresses tonic dopamine modulation of global precision rather than the local, phasic variance computation REE proposes (E3-derived variance from a specific predictive model).
The affordance/action-selection framing is specific to motor hierarchy; the REE BetaGate operates at a cognitive control level that is not directly modelled.
Simulated Parkinson's-like deficits are presented as validation, but this requires the transfer from pathological to healthy-optimal to REE agent which is speculative.

entries.confidence_rationale: Good mapping to the threshold-gating half of ARC-016's circuit: striatum and pre-SMA activation correlates with response threshold adjustment under speed pressure, directly instantiating the claim that a BG-frontal circuit modulates action-selection gating based on a threshold parameter. Individual variation in striatum activation predicts individual variation in threshold — confirming the threshold is a circuit-level variable, not just behavioural. Moderate transfer risk from response-speed pressure to REE's precision-derived commitment threshold.
failure_signatures: The threshold manipulation is externally imposed (speed-accuracy cues) not internally derived from prediction uncertainty, unlike ARC-016's E3-derived variance mechanism.
Pre-SMA/striatum are motor circuit nodes; ARC-016's BetaGate is conceptually at the cognitive control level, requiring an additional inference that the same circuit logic generalises upward.
fMRI temporal resolution cannot resolve whether threshold is set before or during the deliberation phase, which matters for the E3-derived variance → threshold → gate ordering ARC-016 requires.

entries.confidence_rationale: This major theoretical review argues that hippocampal and entorhinal circuits implement a general-purpose relational/structural knowledge map that extends well beyond Euclidean space to organise any domain with relational structure — including value, social hierarchy, and abstract feature space. This supports the spirit of ARC-007 by demonstrating that hippocampal maps are not geometrically constrained but are domain-general structural representations that can in principle encode harm/residue topology. However, the paper is a theoretical synthesis rather than direct experimental evidence for harm-terrain organisation; it broadens the concept of cognitive map without specifically grounding it in salience/harm fields. The 'mixed' direction reflects that the paper both supports the generalisation (maps can encode non-spatial structure) and implicitly challenges the specific REE framing (there is no empirical anchor for harm-residue as a map axis).
failure_signatures: The paper does not provide evidence that harm or causal attribution specifically is used as an organising axis for hippocampal maps — the non-spatial examples are relational/semantic, not harm-weighted.

entries.confidence_rationale: This is the central theoretical paper linking hippocampal place cells to a successor representation (SR) that encodes future-state predictions weighted by transition statistics. The SR is inherently sensitive to reward/value topology: when reward distributions change, the effective map distorts around reward-dense regions. This directly supports ARC-007's claim that hippocampal maps are organised around value/salience terrain rather than pure geometry. The paper is high-quality (Nature Neuroscience, widely cited), but it is primarily a theoretical/computational model with supporting re-analysis of existing datasets rather than novel electrophysiology — hence mapping fidelity is high but the empirical grounding is partly indirect.

This paper provides a normative theory of hippocampal replay that directly links replay content to value/utility structure: memories are replayed in order of their 'need' — how much extra reward would accrue from better decisions based on that memory. This means replay is inherently organised around value topology rather than spatial recency or proximity. For ARC-007, this is directly relevant: if replay is prioritised by utility/value, then high-residue (high-harm) locations would be candidates for prioritised replay when they are proximate in a planned trajectory, providing a computational mechanism for how paths through residue-field terrain are differentially stored and reactivated. The theory unifies planning, consolidation, and learning functions of replay under a single value-sensitive framework. Confidence is slightly reduced because the empirical validation is against spatial navigation data where 'value' is reward, not harm/residue.

This paper provides direct electrophysiological evidence that hippocampal theta sequences (time-compressed path representations) are dynamically modulated by the animal's current goal location and the value structure of the task. Look-ahead distance scales with goal distance, predicts the animal's destination on single trials, and varies moment-by-moment with behavioural context. This strongly supports ARC-007: hippocampal sequences are not passively replaying geometry but are actively shaped by goal/value topology. The paper is novel in-vivo electrophysiology in rats performing a genuine value-guided decision task. Confidence is slightly below the Stachenfeld paper because the direct connection to 'harm salience terrain' requires interpretive translation — the paper demonstrates goal-sensitivity of sequences, not explicit residue/harm-field organisation.

Landmark in-vivo demonstration that hippocampal place-cell sequences during awake replay project from the animal's current position to a remembered goal location -- an explicit forward sweep, not just a sequential replay. The sweeps are goal-directed and terminate at the reward site, which directly supports the idea that hippocampal rollouts are initialized from current state and propagated toward a target. Confidence slightly below Wikenheiser & Redish because the seeding mechanism is not identified and the sequences here occur during awake stillness (sharp wave ripples) rather than during active deliberation theta sequences.

Direct electrophysiological evidence that hippocampal place-cell sequences 'decouple' from the animal's current position to represent future paths during deliberation. This is the closest empirical analog to rollout seeding: the sequence is not a replay of past experience but a prospective projection. Confidence is high because this is in-vivo single-unit recording during active decision-making, not just a computational model. Not higher because (a) the paper does not identify the circuit source of the decoupled sequence -- it does not show that a forward-predictor analogous to E2 seeds the sequence, and (b) the task is spatial navigation with explicit decision points, not abstract goal-harm planning.

Strong theoretical framework showing hippocampus encodes successor representations (SR) -- a predictive map of future states -- which maps closely onto E2-seeded rollout initialisation. The mechanism is well-specified and the SR framing directly supports the claim that hippocampal trajectory rollouts are initialized from a forward-predictive representation. Confidence is not higher because the paper is a computational/theoretical model fitted to rodent place-field data, not a direct mechanistic demonstration that a fast forward predictor (E2 analogue) provides the initial state vector.

Hippocampal preplay literature provides evidence that the hippocampus can generate place-cell sequences for environments that have never been experienced -- suggesting the sequences are not purely replays of past transitions but can be seeded by something other than episodic memory. This is moderately supportive of MECH-033 (E2 kernels as the seed source), but equally consistent with the view that the sequences arise from intrinsic CA3 attractor dynamics or random statistical structure in the population. Confidence is moderate-low because the preplay phenomenon is itself contested (some accounts attribute it to chance alignment of random sequences with future experience), and the causal link to a forward model seeding mechanism is indirect.
failure_signatures: Preplay sequences are statistically indistinguishable from random population activity in some analyses, raising the possibility that apparent 'preplay' reflects post-hoc alignment rather than genuine prospective computation -- which would undercut the rollout-seeding interpretation.

entries.confidence_rationale: This is the canonical theoretical framework for LC-NE function: the paper explicitly proposes that LC phasic activity (triggered by task-relevant unexpected events, including negative outcomes) mediates behavioral flexibility by transiently raising gain and facilitating disengagement from the current task/behavioral mode. The adaptive gain theory maps well onto MECH-104's volatility interrupt enabling de-commitment. Confidence docked slightly because the paper is a theoretical synthesis rather than direct causal manipulation, and the specific link to harm surprise vs. other salient events is not cleanly separated.

Bouret and Sara's 'network reset' framework is the most direct mechanistic proposal in the literature for what MECH-104 calls a volatility interrupt. They explicitly argue that phasic LC-NE activity triggered by unexpected, motivationally significant events produces a transient reset of cortical network dynamics -- clearing the current attractor state and allowing rapid transitions to a new behavioral mode. This is the 'enabling de-commitment' component of MECH-104 stated in nearly its exact terms. Confidence is slightly below Aston-Jones/Cohen because this is a shorter perspective/theory piece with less empirical detail, and the reset metaphor, while evocative, is not formalised as precisely as Yu and Dayan's Bayesian treatment.

Sara and Bouret provide substantial mechanistic grounding for MECH-104 from two angles: (1) direct LC unit recording demonstrating reliable LC activation to unexpected salient events including aversive ones, with firing preceding behavioral reorienting by 50-100ms; (2) a clear proposal that LC-mediated NE release in prefrontal and ACC drives cognitive flexibility -- specifically the ability to disengage from the current behavioral context and reorient. The paper also documents the LC projections to ACC that are most relevant to REE's commitment-gating circuit. Confidence is slightly below the Yu/Dayan entry because the mechanism is described at the level of 'cognitive flexibility' and 'reorienting' rather than formalised as uncertainty signalling, leaving more interpretive work for the REE mapping.

Yu and Dayan make the most explicit theoretical case in the literature for a clean dissociation between expected uncertainty (tracked by ACh) and unexpected uncertainty (tracked by NE). Unexpected uncertainty -- defined as uncertainty arising from violations of the generative model itself, i.e., the world has changed -- is proposed to drive NE-mediated attentional reorienting. This maps with high fidelity onto MECH-104's claim that unexpected harm (a prime trigger of unexpected uncertainty) elevates NE and enables de-commitment. Confidence docked because this is a computational model with limited direct LC-NE causal data; the ACh/NE dissociation is an inference from behavioral pharmacology rather than direct recording.

Ito's argument that cerebellar internal models reproduce essential properties of *mental representations in the cerebral cortex* -- not merely motor dynamics -- is strong indirect support for MECH-135. If the cerebellum models cortical representation dynamics, then cerebellar prediction (E2 in REE terms) must be coupled to cortical state (E1/z_world) during mental simulation, not just during motor execution. This logically implies a form of parallel co-evolution. However, the paper is a theoretical review without direct experimental demonstration of simultaneous E2-E1 state co-evolution, and the mechanism proposed is general enough that sequential architectures are not excluded.
failure_signatures: Ito's framework does not specify whether cerebellar internal models run simultaneously with cortical state updates or whether they run in an interleaved/sequential fashion. The co-evolution timing claim in MECH-135 is not directly addressed.

entries.confidence_rationale: This paper provides the strongest biological grounding among the neuroscience entries for the parallel coupling claim. The Tanaka et al. review presents three converging lines of evidence: (1) dentate nucleus output is predictive of mossy fiber input (physiological), (2) movement kinematics are predictive in healthy subjects but lagging in cerebellar ataxia patients (behavioral), and (3) cerebellar cortex and dentate nucleus receive separate mossy fiber projections encoding cortical-descent and sensory-feedback signals respectively -- a morphological prerequisite for a Kalman filter computation. The Kalman filter framing is particularly relevant: a Kalman filter is inherently a parallel process (prediction step + update step simultaneously tracking a state estimate), which maps cleanly onto MECH-135's claim that E2 and E1 must co-evolve. The morphological finding of separate MF channels also supports the z_self/z_world split in SD-005. Confidence is capped at 0.74 because the paper is in the motor domain and the Kalman filter interpretation, while compelling, remains a theoretical framing rather than a direct demonstration of co-evolution.
failure_signatures: The paper's evidence for Kalman-filter-style parallel computation is primarily in the motor domain; the extension to cognitive/planning functions is speculative and not demonstrated.

The separate MF projection finding is morphological and compatible with sequential gating architectures as well as parallel ones -- it is a necessary but not sufficient condition for parallel co-evolution.

entries.confidence_rationale: GRASP provides a computational existence proof that parallel latent state optimization during planning rollouts is both feasible and superior to sequential shooting methods. The 'lifted states' formulation -- treating each timestep's latent state as an independent optimization variable -- is structurally analogous to allowing z_world to co-evolve across trajectory steps rather than being fixed. However, GRASP operates within a single world model rather than coupling two distinct models (E2 and E1), so it does not directly test the two-model co-evolution claim in MECH-135. The parallel advantage demonstrated (up to +10% success rate at half compute cost) is relevant evidence that parallel latent computation during planning is practically beneficial, which is consistent with MECH-135's motivation. The preprint status and single-model architecture limit confidence.
failure_signatures: GRASP uses a single world model with parallelized state optimization across time -- it does not involve two separate models (fast/cerebellar and slow/cortical) co-evolving. The dual-model structure of MECH-135 is not tested.

The 'lifted states' formulation with soft dynamics constraints is a gradient-based optimization technique, not a biological parallel computation -- the mapping to cerebellar-cortical co-evolution requires significant additional bridging.

entries.confidence_rationale: MOSAIC architecture explicitly proposes multiple paired forward models running in parallel, with each forward model predicting consequences while responsibility estimates gate their contributions. This is a direct structural analogue to the E2 (cerebellar/predictive) running parallel to E1 (cortical/world-state) during planning. However, the biological paper does not address co-evolution of a slower world model during the rollout -- it assumes the cortical context is given, not co-updating -- so the parallel structure is confirmed but the co-evolution claim requires further support.
failure_signatures: The MOSAIC framework treats context/environment as an input to responsibility estimators, not as a co-evolving world-state updated by cerebellar output during the rollout. This is a structural gap relative to MECH-135's co-evolution claim.

entries.confidence_rationale: Authoritative review in Science synthesising the dual-pathway framework for pain affect, explicitly distinguishing primary unpleasantness from secondary affect (anticipatory/emotional), and detailing both direct spinal-limbic pathways and cortico-limbic pathways converging on ACC. Provides the explicit circuit-level architecture that SD-011 instantiates. High source quality and strong mapping fidelity, but as a review it does not add new primary experimental data beyond the literature it synthesises.

Systematic meta-analysis of 68 hemodynamic pain studies in normal subjects and 30 in clinical pain, synthesising the brain network for pain. Directly identifies S1, S2, insula, ACC, and PFC as the core pain matrix, with explicit discussion of functional roles. Provides population-level consistency data for the sensory-discriminative vs. affective-motivational split across many studies, strengthening the generalisability of the two-stream claim. Lower individual entry confidence than Rainville because it is a broad review/meta-analysis rather than a focused dissociation experiment.

Craig's foundational NRN review provides the anatomical substrate for the dual-stream distinction by establishing the lamina I spinothalamic pathway to posterior insula as the dedicated sensory-discriminative interoceptive route, while describing the medial spinothalamic projections to affective-motivational cortex. This paper is the definitive account of the lateral vs. medial STT distinction in primates and humans, and is the most influential articulation of where in the brain the two streams physically separate.

Canonical human PET study using hypnotic modulation to selectively alter pain unpleasantness while holding intensity constant. Demonstrates functional dissociation between ACC (affect) and S1 (intensity/sensory-discriminative), directly instantiating the two-stream split that SD-011 encodes as z_harm_a vs z_harm_s. High source quality (Science, >5000 citations), clean experimental design, direct mapping to claim.

Comprehensive anatomical review of the spinothalamic tract and other ascending nociceptive pathways by two of the leading tract-tracing neuroanatomists of the 20th century. Directly describes the lateral vs. medial spinothalamic tract division, their distinct thalamic targets, and their functional significance for sensory-discriminative vs. affective-motivational pain processing. This is the definitive anatomical foundation for the proposed subcortical stream separation in SD-011.

Provides neural evidence for a distinct causal-inference gating mechanism that modulates how outcomes are attributed to agent actions versus external causes. RPEs modulated by causal beliefs are represented in dorsal striatum (action-learning pathway) while unmodulated RPEs are in ventral striatum. Causal beliefs are represented in anterior insula, with effective connectivity from insula to dorsal striatum. This is the neural implementation of the attribution asymmetry that MECH-071 predicts: the brain has a dedicated circuit for deciding whether an outcome was caused by the agent and gating learning accordingly. Confidence 0.72 because the paper is about reward, not harm, and uses standard RL credit assignment rather than harm-specific prediction evaluation.
failure_signatures: If E2/E3 harm prediction errors are not differentially routed based on causal attribution (agent-caused vs environment-caused), the dorsal/ventral striatum dissociation predicted by this paper would not appear in REE's architecture.
If the insula-to-striatum gating of causal beliefs is absent in harm-specific circuits, MECH-071's calibration asymmetry would need a different implementation route.

entries.confidence_rationale: Directly demonstrates the central mechanism that makes MECH-071 neurobiologically plausible: cerebellar forward models predict the sensory consequences of self-generated movement and attenuate the somatosensory cortical response. External stimulation produces greater somatosensory cortex activation than identical self-produced stimulation, establishing that the brain differentially encodes self-generated versus externally-generated sensory events. The mapping to E2 as efference-copy forward model and the self/environment asymmetry in harm prediction is direct. Confidence capped at 0.80 because the domain is tactile sensation (not harm evaluation) and the stimuli are benign.
failure_signatures: If E2 cannot distinguish self-generated from environment-generated harm events at the latent level, this cerebellar forward-model architecture would predict identical somatosensory responses -- the opposite of what the paper shows.

entries.confidence_rationale: The paper provides the most direct behavioral evidence for MECH-071's self/environment calibration asymmetry. When subjects match a force they have experienced, they systematically overestimate the force needed when reproducing it via their own action -- because the self-generated force feels weaker (attenuated by efference copy prediction). This means self-generated forces are UNDER-perceived relative to externally-applied identical forces. Applied to harm: agent-caused harm events produce weaker afferent harm signals than environmentally-caused harm events of identical physical magnitude. E2 operating as a forward model for self-generated actions would therefore receive systematically attenuated harm feedback compared to environment-caused events -- this is precisely the calibration asymmetry MECH-071 predicts. The confidence is 0.82 -- strong behavioral signal, clear mechanism, but domain remains force/tactile rather than harm evaluation proper.
failure_signatures: If E2 harm prediction shows no agent/environment differential in calibration quality under identical physical harm magnitudes, the sensory attenuation mechanism this paper establishes would be disconfirmed as a route to MECH-071.
If the calibration asymmetry flips -- E2 better calibrated for environment-caused harm -- this would require a different theoretical account.

entries.confidence_rationale: Directly demonstrates the graded threat-proximity neural encoding that MECH-071's second claim (E3 learns a graded danger model, approach gradient not just contact) requires. As a virtual predator approached subjects in an avoidance maze, brain activity systematically shifted from vmPFC (distal threat, planning-dominated) to PAG (proximal threat, defensive escape). The gradient is continuous and prospective -- the brain encodes danger before contact, as a function of proximity. This is the neural instantiation of the approach-gradient harm model. Confidence 0.77: the effect is robust and in human brain during active avoidance, which maps well to E3's harm evaluation during trajectory planning. The virtual predator paradigm is somewhat artificial and the gradient is measured in BOLD not in latent harm representations.
failure_signatures: If E3 only represents harm at contact (binary) without any prospective graded encoding during approach, the vmPFC-to-PAG gradient seen in this paper would have no REE analogue -- MECH-071's graded danger model would be disconfirmed.
If the approach-gradient structure in z_harm_s is flat until contact, this paper's continuous proximity-coding would predict that result cannot replicate in REE's architecture.

entries.confidence_rationale: Olafsdottir et al. demonstrate that awake hippocampal replay dynamically switches between task-focused (planning-mode) and consolidation-mode reactivation depending on task demands. Replay immediately before or after movement toward a reward preferentially encoded the current trajectory; during extended immobility, bias disappeared. Task-focused reactivations predicted subsequent decision accuracy. This is direct evidence that hippocampal replay is a context-sensitive, decision-relevant process -- supporting ARC-018's claim that the viability map is actively maintained and queried at decision time rather than being a static structure. The switch between modes (planning vs consolidation) also supports the hypothesis that the E3 harm/goal error signal gates replay content.

Gold-standard electrophysiology in behaving rats demonstrating that hippocampal place-cell sequences generated before goal-directed navigation depict future goal-directed trajectories. The sequences are goal-biased (current location to known goal) and predict immediate future behaviour even for novel start-goal combinations. This is direct evidence for the hippocampus generating prospective rollouts indexed by a destination (goal) coordinate. The main caveat for ARC-018 is that the indexing dimension here is spatial location rather than the E2 action-object coordinate posited in the V3 framing; the biology supports the rollout mechanism but is agnostic about what the coordinate system encodes at a computational level.

Dolan & Dayan's comprehensive review of goal-directed vs habitual decision-making provides the theoretical scaffolding that situates ARC-018's hippocampal viability map. They articulate the model-based/model-free distinction, clarify that goal-directed behaviour requires a cognitive map whose value is updated prospectively (not merely retrospectively), and review the evidence that hippocampus is an essential substrate for model-based planning. This is theoretical grounding rather than direct experimental evidence, hence the lower confidence -- but the conceptual alignment is high. The review's framing of 'model-based' as requiring explicit forward simulation is exactly what ARC-018 claims for the viability map. Confidence is limited by the fact that ARC-018 makes specific mechanistic claims (E2 indexing, E3 error update) that go well beyond what this review can address.

Momennejad et al. provide direct human fMRI evidence that off-task hippocampal replay (indexed by multi-voxel pattern reactivation) predicts subsequent replanning behaviour, and that uncertainty (unsigned prediction error) triggers such replay. This maps closely onto ARC-018's claim that the hippocampal viability map is updated by E3 harm/goal error: here, prediction error (a proxy for uncertainty/harm-goal discrepancy) triggers the hippocampal reactivation that enables replanning. The human context is also valuable -- it extends the rodent findings across species. The main limitation for ARC-018 is that the task is abstract (two-step sequential decision) rather than spatial navigation, which is actually a strength: it suggests the mechanism generalises beyond spatial maps to action-object level representations.

Mattar & Daw provide the clearest computational framework for why hippocampal replay is ordered by utility -- specifically, by how much additional reward would be earned from better choices at each state. This 'prioritized memory access' framework maps closely onto ARC-018's viability map: the hippocampal representation is updated in proportion to its relevance to current decision-making under harm/goal error signals. The paper also proposes that dysfunction of this prioritisation mechanism underlies rumination and craving -- a striking parallel to the harm-valence component of ARC-018's viability map. Confidence slightly below peak because this is a theoretical/computational model, not electrophysiology, and the utility metric used (reward gain) is not identical to E3 harm/goal error.

Axelrod's iterated prisoner's dilemma framework is one of the most rigorous formal models of when cooperation breaks down and defection (analogous to conflict/violence) becomes the equilibrium strategy. The core finding -- that cooperation persists when the shadow of the future is long enough, and collapses when repeat interaction is no longer expected -- maps directly onto MECH-102's claim that violence emerges when coordination pathways are unavailable. Axelrod's 'provocability' condition (immediate retaliation to defection) and 'forgiveness' condition (return to cooperation after defection is punished) together imply a threshold structure where escalation to high-energy conflict is reserved for cases where the cooperative equilibrium cannot be restored. Confidence is moderate-high because the formal structure is compelling, but the translation from iterated PD to REE's error-correction framework requires non-trivial interpretation.

Berkowitz's reformulation directly frames aggression/violence as triggered by goal-path blockage (pathway exhaustion), with negative affect as the mediating mechanism. This maps plausibly onto MECH-102's claim that violence is triggered when coordination pathways become unavailable. The fit is strongest for the individual level. Transfer risk is moderate because Berkowitz is modelling emotional aggression in individuals, not architectural error-correction in systems, and his model does not distinguish low-energy vs. high-energy intervention paths in the REE sense.

The General Aggression Model (GAM) provides a comprehensive unifying framework for human aggression that is relevant but ambivalent toward MECH-102. The GAM posits that aggression emerges from interaction of situational inputs and person variables, processed through cognition, affect, and arousal. This is consistent with MECH-102 in that it acknowledges aggression is not the default response -- it requires triggering conditions. However, the GAM does not specifically frame violence as 'terminal' or require exhaustion of low-energy pathways before aggression is selected. The GAM's episodic appraisal and reappraisal component is the strongest connection: it implies that aggressive action is selected after evaluation of available responses, which allows for a reading consistent with MECH-102's escalation ladder. Confidence is moderate because the mapping requires selective reading of the GAM.
failure_signatures: The GAM does not require prior exhaustion of non-violent coordination alternatives before aggression is triggered -- it can be activated by hostile attribution bias, provocation cues, or script activation without pathway closure.

entries.confidence_rationale: Aloyo's paper is directly about the 'last resort' criterion in just war theory, making it superficially highly relevant to MECH-102. However, Aloyo's central conclusion is that the last resort criterion is either redundant with proportionality or produces perverse recommendations -- and should therefore be dropped. This is a complication rather than a simple support or weakening of MECH-102. The complication is philosophically important: if 'last resort' is not a coherent standalone criterion at the institutional/civilizational level, then MECH-102's claim that violence is 'triggered only when low-energy coordination pathways have been exhausted' faces the same philosophical challenge Aloyo raises. On the other hand, Aloyo's preferred replacement (minimize severe harms to innocents) is structurally consistent with INV-029 (love as long-horizon coherence bias) -- the real work is done by coherence-preservation, not by an ordinal sequencing of violence as 'last' in a temporal sense.
failure_signatures: Aloyo argues that all accounts of last resort either generate wrong policy recommendations or reduce to proportionality, suggesting the 'sequential exhaustion' framing of MECH-102 may face analogous philosophical problems at the institutional level.

entries.confidence_rationale: This review paper makes the key generalisation that the cognitive map is not a spatial map but a map of task-relevant relational structure -- which directly licenses SD-004's claim that the HippocampalModule should map action-object space rather than raw z_world. Behrens et al. argue that place and grid cells are a specific instance of general coding principles for organising any structured knowledge. The non-spatial cognitive map framing is exactly what SD-004 requires: the map backbone is action-consequence structure, not geometric coordinates.
failure_signatures: Paper remains largely descriptive of biological findings; the abstraction to action-object space is inferred from the logic of generalisation, not demonstrated experimentally in a non-spatial task with action-object structure
The authors note that direct neural evidence for non-spatial cognitive maps is still emerging; the strongest evidence remains spatial

entries.confidence_rationale: This is the primary computational paper arguing that hippocampal place cells encode a predictive (successor) map rather than purely geometric space. The SR framework directly instantiates the core SD-004 claim that the hippocampal map is organized by future-state structure -- i.e., by what follows from current position under a given policy -- rather than raw sensory coordinates. The mapping to SD-004 requires one extension (from state-successor to action-consequence objects), but the foundational architecture is essentially identical. High-quality journal, large citation base, replicated predictions.
failure_signatures: SR is policy-dependent -- the predictive map changes when behavior changes, implying the map backbone is not a stable action-object library but a policy-contingent weighting
SR as originally formulated does not compress multi-step action sequences into objects; it represents individual states weighted by future occupancy

entries.confidence_rationale: This is the foundational paper introducing the successor representation -- the computational substrate that Stachenfeld et al. (2017) later linked to hippocampal place cells. Dayan shows that representing states by their expected future state-occupancies (discounted, policy-weighted) enables efficient generalisation and long-horizon value computation without per-step simulation. This directly grounds SD-004's planning-horizon claim: action objects as SR units collapse multi-step sequences into single entries, giving the same computational advantage Dayan demonstrated for temporally extended state representations. Confidence below 0.85 because the original paper predates neural binding; the hippocampal link is established by subsequent work.
failure_signatures: SR is defined over primitive states; Dayan does not consider action-object compression -- the extension to action-consequence chunks requires additional theoretical work (temporal abstraction, options framework)
SR remains policy-dependent; when the agent's policy changes, the SR must be relearned -- this raises the question of whether action objects can serve as a stable map backbone across policy updates

entries.confidence_rationale: This paper provides direct electrophysiological evidence that hippocampal sequential activity is not a passive replay of recent spatial experience but a goal-directed prospective computation: look-ahead distance varies with the distance to the current goal. This supports SD-004's requirement that the HippocampalModule's traversal of action-object space be goal-directed rather than purely state-driven. The paper also suggests that planning horizon scales with goal distance -- exactly the long-horizon planning advantage SD-004 claims for action-object navigation. Confidence reduced from peak because the paper operates in raw place-cell space, not action-object space.
failure_signatures: Sequences are of spatial positions, not compressed action objects -- the goal-scaling effect is demonstrated for spatial look-ahead, not for chunked multi-step action sequences

Look-ahead in theta sequences is typically a few tens of centimetres, far shorter than the 20-step planning horizon SD-004 targets; longer-horizon planning may require sharp-wave ripples rather than theta sequences

entries.confidence_rationale: This paper establishes that midbrain dopamine neurons encode a temporal-difference reward prediction error (RPE) — a signal that is positive for unexpected rewards, negative for predicted reward omissions, and zero for fully predicted rewards. This operationalises 'surprise' as a distinct, neurally separable signal from hedonic impact and incentive salience. For ARC-036, this grounds the surprise dimension of the hippocampal valence terrain: unexpected events (rewarding or aversive) generate RPE signals that gate state-space updating. The paper provides electrophysiological confirmation that the DA system carries a signal matching the theoretical RPE of temporal-difference models, establishing surprise as a genuine fourth dimension separable from wanting and liking. Confidence is high; slight reduction reflects that the hippocampal terrain-map connection is inferential — DA RPE acts as a learning signal that modulates what gets encoded in terrain, not a direct terrain dimension itself.

This 2009 review consolidates and extends the wanting/liking dissociation with direct anatomical localization of hedonic hotspots. Critically, it maps discrete subregions of nucleus accumbens shell and ventral pallidum where mu-opioid or endocannabinoid stimulation amplifies liking reactions, while surrounding regions amplify wanting without affecting liking. This provides a spatially resolved, substrate-specific grounding for the liking dimension of ARC-036's multi-dimensional valence map. For ARC-036, the finding that liking is encoded in anatomically discrete hotspots — not diffusely distributed — supports the claim that valence dimensions have separable substrate identity, which is a prerequisite for hippocampal terrain encoding of them as distinct channels. Confidence is slightly lower than the 1998 paper because this review's scope is pharmacological rather than directly relevant to hippocampal navigation.

Mattar and Daw's prioritized replay framework provides the mechanism by which a multi-dimensional valence terrain would be differentially consolidated and updated in hippocampus. The paper demonstrates that replay content is determined by a value-based utility function (need x gain) rather than recency or spatial proximity. For ARC-036, this means that if hippocampus encodes separable valence dimensions (wanting, liking, harm, surprise), then the terrain zones with high utility across any of those dimensions would be candidates for prioritized replay. The paper further supports the claim that replay is not a passive echo of experience but an active maintenance process that sculpts the terrain map according to decision-relevant value structure. This is a different mapping from the ARC-007 entry (which focuses on residue-field path prioritization); here the focus is on how value-weighted replay supports the multi-dimensional terrain structure that ARC-036 claims hippocampus maintains.

This is the canonical paper establishing that dopamine subserves 'wanting' (incentive salience) but not 'liking' (hedonic impact), and that reward learning (prediction error updating) is a third functionally separable process. The dissociation is demonstrated across multiple lesion, pharmacological, and behavioural paradigms in rodents. For ARC-036, this is the foundational paper grounding the claim that the hippocampal terrain map encodes separable valence dimensions: wanting and liking are shown to be doubly dissociable both behaviourally and neurobiologically, establishing that a terrain map tracking both would require at minimum two distinct valence channels. Confidence is high because the evidence base is large and the dissociation is robust; slight reduction from 1.0 reflects that this paper does not directly address hippocampal encoding or replay.

The PBWM model provides the canonical computational instantiation of parallel, functionally distinct BG-PFC loops in which distinct error signals (dopamine prediction error, working memory maintenance signal, output gating signal) drive separate learning channels. The paper demonstrates that collapsing these channels -- making the update and maintenance gate the same -- produces a recognisable failure mode: the system cannot hold information stable while simultaneously updating other representations. This is the computational signature of what ARC-021 asserts: separate learning channels are necessary because mixing error signals produces misattribution. The limitation is that PBWM focuses on two parallel PFC stripes (maintenance and output gating) and does not cleanly decompose into the three-loop anatomy (sensorimotor / associative / limbic) that grounds ARC-021. The mapping requires the reader to equate the computational channels with biological loop anatomy, which the paper does not make explicit.
failure_signatures: The paper's distinct channels correspond to working memory maintenance vs output gating in PFC, not directly to the three corticostriatal loops (sensorimotor, associative, limbic). ARC-021 requires the three-loop anatomy; PBWM requires the two-gating-channel computational argument.

If the three biological loops can be reduced to a common dopamine RL signal without functional loss, the learning-channel-separation claim weakens -- PBWM itself uses a single DA signal that gates both channels.

entries.confidence_rationale: Camille et al. (2004) provides a double dissociation argument: patients with orbitofrontal cortex lesions fail to report regret and fail to anticipate negative consequences of committed choices, while normal subjects do both. This lesion study establishes that post-commitment counterfactual evaluation is an active, dedicated neural computation -- not merely an implicit process -- and that its absence measurably changes decision behavior. When the circuit is removed by lesion, patients make suboptimal choices that normal subjects learn to avoid through accumulated regret. This is structural evidence that committed-mode error processing is distinct from pre-commitment deliberation: the OFC is required for post-commitment evaluation, and agents without it behave differently in measurable ways. ARC-029 claims that committed and uncommitted modes produce measurably distinct harm outcomes; the lesion evidence provides the double-dissociation side of this argument -- that there exists a neural mechanism specifically dedicated to post-commitment outcome evaluation, and its presence or absence changes behavioral outcomes. Confidence moderated by the clinical/computational gap and by the fact that the paper measures choice suboptimality (regret-avoidance failure) rather than harm accumulation directly.
failure_signatures: OFC lesion patients still make decisions and incur outcomes -- they do not become unable to operate. The deficit is specifically in regret-learning and regret anticipation, not in general decision capacity. This means the committed/uncommitted distinction is specifically about the evaluative and learning circuit, not about execution capacity. ARC-029's claim about distinct harm outcomes would need to specify whether it is about the execution or the evaluation channel.

The paper is a clinical case series with small N -- a typical limitation for lesion studies. The specific OFC lesion locations vary across patients. The generalisation from OFC lesion patients to a computational architecture requires strong assumptions about modularity.

entries.confidence_rationale: Coricelli et al. (2005) demonstrates that committed choices (where the outcome of the unchosen alternative is revealed) produce qualitatively different neural and behavioral responses than unchosen alternatives: specifically, medial OFC and anterior cingulate activate in proportion to the counterfactual gap between what was obtained and what could have been obtained. Subjects became progressively regret-aversive over the experiment, and the same OFC/amygdala circuit that registered experienced regret was active prospectively during decision-making, modifying subsequent choices to avoid regret. This is the neural signature of a committed-mode error routing: post-commitment outcomes (realised consequences of chosen actions) are evaluated against a counterfactual baseline in a way that influences future committed choices differently from pre-commitment deliberation. ARC-029 claims that committed and uncommitted operating modes produce measurably distinct harm outcomes. The Coricelli paper supports the distinct modes claim: committed outcomes activate a neural circuit (OFC/ACC/amygdala regret system) that is not active during pre-decision simulation, and this circuit specifically evaluates irreversible consequences. Confidence moderated by the significant mapping gap: Coricelli studies human subjective regret in a gambling paradigm, while ARC-029 is about observable harm accumulation in an agent control loop.
failure_signatures: The paper studies subjective emotional responses (regret as a felt state) rather than objective harm accumulation differences. ARC-029's claim is specifically about measurably distinct harm outcomes -- a behavioral rather than subjective claim. The Coricelli paper does not measure harm directly and does not compare cumulative harm between committed and uncommitted conditions.

The gambling paradigm used by Coricelli does not involve the kind of irreversible physical harm that motivates ARC-029's commit boundary. Losing money in a gamble is financially irreversible but qualitatively different from the harm-irreversibility that motivates the pre/post-commit distinction in REE.

entries.confidence_rationale: Hyman et al. provide simultaneous recordings of hippocampal and mPFC theta during spatial navigation, showing that mPFC theta power and coherence with hippocampus are elevated during periods of active spatial navigation and working memory demands. The finding that theta coordinates information flow between these structures during navigation supports ARC-032's architectural claim. The 2010 Hippocampus paper complements Benchenane 2010 by characterizing the oscillatory properties during ongoing navigation rather than around a learning event, providing evidence that theta packaging is a persistent property of the goal-navigation communication channel rather than only a learning signal.
failure_signatures: If mPFC-hippocampal theta coherence during navigation reflects hippocampal movement signaling rather than frontal goal representation, the ARC-032 framing would be inverted
If theta appears during any locomotion regardless of goal-directedness, the goal-context specificity of the packaging claim would be undermined
